frozen_components: small-scale base model (not trained in this work)
architecture.summary: The paper trains reward models (RMs) to evaluate step-by-step solutions to math problems. Two supervision approaches are compared: outcome supervision (ORM), which labels only final correctness, and process supervision (PRM), which labels the correctness of each intermediate reasoning step. Both RM types are finetuned from GPT-4 and trained on solutions generated by a fixed generator model. At test time, a RM scores candidate solutions via best-of-N ranking, selecting the highest-scored solution and checking its final answer. The core comparison isolates whether step-level feedback (PRM) or final-answer feedback (ORM) produces more reliable reward models.

claims.statement: Our process-supervised model solves 78% of problems from a representative subset of the MATH test set.

Process supervision significantly outperforms outcome supervision for training models to solve problems from the challenging MATH dataset. The PRM reaches higher performance for all values of N, and the performance gap widens as N increases.

baseline.name: outcome supervision (two variants: PRM-based and final-answer)

claims.statement: Process supervision significantly outperforms both forms of outcome supervision at all data collection scales (1 to 200 solutions per problem).
Active learning leads to approximately 2.6x improvement in the data efficiency of process supervision compared to uniform data labelling.
The PRM outperforms both the ORM and majority voting on out-of-distribution STEM tests (AP Physics, AP Calculus, AP Chemistry, AMC10/12) with 100 test samples per problem.

Process supervision makes credit assignment easier because it specifies both how many of the first steps were correct as well as the precise location of the incorrect step, compared to outcome supervision which faces a difficult credit-assignment task of determining where an incorrect solution went wrong.

Process supervision incurs a negative alignment tax: it is both safer and more performant than outcome supervision. Process supervision is more likely to produce interpretable reasoning and directly rewards aligned chain-of-thought.
We release PRM800K, the complete dataset of 800,000 step-level human feedback labels used to train our best reward model.
